Gobowen Station Community Enhancement Project   Gobowen Station awarded £246k grant for building repairs Published 25 September 2023 Share MP Helen Morgan Image source,Helen Morgan Image caption, Helen Morgan, MP for North Shropshire, said the grant was "great news" for Gobowen Shehnaz Khan BBC News, West Midlands Historic listed buildings at a railway station are set to be restored following a grant from the government. A £246,146 grant was awarded to Shropshire's Gobowen Station through the Community Ownership Fund. Money will be spent on external repairs and improvements to buildings that flank both platforms. The station is among a number of projects nationwide to benefit from more than £150m in funding., external Helen Morgan, MP for North Shropshire, said the grant was "great news" and a "welcome boost" for station staff and volunteers. "Oswestry Station Building Trust deserves a big round of applause for this success," she said. She added the next step was to reopen the railway link from Gobowen to Oswestry. The line was shut in 1966 during the Beeching cuts, which saw thousands of stations closed and lines axed nationwide. In 2022, plans were presented to the government for a light railway to link Oswestry with Gobowen Station, which has recently celebrated its 175th anniversary. A decision on the proposals was due by spring this year but has been delayed. "The outline business case is excellent and it's now down to the government to end the delays and get the wheels turning on improving Shropshire's transport links," Ms Morgan stated. In August, the Department for Transport said: "We're currently reviewing proposals for our Restoring Your Railway programme,, external which aims to deliver schemes that benefit local communities, and we expect to announce next steps later in the year."

Project_name: Blandford Forum Corn Exchange Restoration and renewal of a community asset
Text: Blandford Forum Corn Exchange: Restoration and renewal of a community asset   Town Hall / Corn Exchange Refurbishment Project To keep up to date with project progress, please view the updates below. You can also visit the Meetings page where you can scroll down to the agendas and minutes for the Corn Exchange meetings. These meetings are usually held in the Corn Exchange, and the public are welcome to attend, however they are being held in Woodhouse Gardens from April 2024 onwards. Victorian Tile Sponsorship Opportunity Victorian Tile Be a part of the town’s heritage Following the Great Fire of Blandford in 1731, the Town Hall and Shambles were designed and constructed by the Bastard brothers in 1734. The Corn Exchange was added later, in 1858, and in 1891 the hall was richly decorated with faience tiles. These decorative tiles have been concealed beneath plywood boxing for several decades, and we now look forward to their restoration and reinstatement as part of the refurbishment of the town’s stunning Grade I Listed building, which is the jewel in the crown of Blandford Forum. A specialist consultant in historic tiling assessed the condition of the tiles and provided recommendations for their repair and replacement. There are 23 different types of tile, with around 1,200 in total, and the restoration process is expected to take nearly 1,000 hours to complete. Among the decorative details is the face of Demeter, the Greek goddess of agriculture, featured on some of the tiles. As a revered figure in ancient Greek religion and the deity of harvest, grain, and fertility, her presence is especially fitting in the Corn Exchange, where corn was traded in the 19th century. The Town Council is pleased to offer the community a unique opportunity to be part of this historic building’s restoration by sponsoring a tile. Whether you’re an individual, business, group, or organisation, you can leave your mark on the Corn Exchange. The name you choose will be proudly included in a list of sponsors on display within the building. Tiles can be sponsored for £30 (including VAT) and can be purchased using this secure online payment form. Alternatively, you may pay by card or cash in the office, or by bank transfer. The Town Council’s bank details are Blandford Forum Town Council – 30-90-92 / 00489333 – please ensure you also email us to confirm payment and your chosen name to appear in the printed display. Please let us know if you require an invoice or receipt for your records. Project Summary The Town Council has been working on a proposal to restore and reinvigorate the Grade I Listed Town Hall and Corn Exchange building for several years.  A planning application was submitted at the start of 2023, which has now been determined and approved. Fundraising work has been ongoing over the last 24+ months and the works themselves have now been tendered. Numerous proposals to repair and revive the Corn Exchange have been and gone over the last 40 years and none have successfully come to fruition. In some cases ideas were perhaps over-ambitious and in other cases they did not pay sufficient attention to the historic significance of the building. All the while, the condition of the building has been deteriorating with repairs being deferred with the result that today there are regular instances of major water ingress due to the poor condition of roofs, extensive damp and various other secondary issues. Compounding this, as every year passes, the building and its facilities have become less and less suitable as the community has changed and the market for venues such as the Corn Exchange – which hasn’t fundamentally changed since the 1950s – has moved on. Essential plant and equipment is now well beyond its useful life, and failing, and large important parts of the building are difficult to let and are effectively obsolete. In 2020-21, the Town Council agreed to take a fresh look at a project to repair and revive the Corn Exchange and confidence, momentum and positivity has slowly been restored. A scheme has been developed focussing on deliverability, not with the aim of fundamentally changing the use of the building but simply making the building do what it has always done but do it better; to become a better multi-purpose community venue. There is now a revived sense of optimism and energy surrounding the project and with the progress towards starting work on site there is real momentum. The proposals will repair and revive the entire Grade I Listed building including the Shambles, Town Hall, Council Chamber and the Corn Exchange. It will breathe new life into the building through a combination of works that will help to increase the use of the building, generate footfall and vibrancy that will provide an anchor for the town centre, support placemaking and ensure that Blandford’s spectacular heart survives and thrives in the future. Specifically, the project will benefit the local community by delivering significant improvements to the Grade I Listed building such as: Delivering a comprehensive programme of conservation repairs. Improving the heating, cooling and acoustics in the Corn Exchange which will make the space more usable for community events and activities. This in turn will increase utilisation and improve the financial sustainability of the building. Installing a new lift and stair to the historic first floor Town Hall and Council Chamber spaces, making these rooms fully accessible for the first time in the building’s 300-year history. This will again help to improve utilisation and financial sustainability. Improving the stage and back-stage facilities for arts and cultural users. This will include providing disabled access to the stage and back-stage areas, new green room facilities and restoring the late Victorian proscenium arch. Repairing and reinstating some of the fine original interiors and removing some of the poor quality more modern interventions, helping to make the interior of the building beautiful again and boosting a local sense of civic pride. The main plans can be viewed here: CEB_MS_L.02A Building Plans As Proposed CEB_MS_L.03A Building Section As Proposed We will continue to keep residents updated with progress made on the project via social media, the Around Blandford free magazines, newsletters and this page. September 2025 This is the final month of works to the building, although there will be a review of snagging issues of course. For events already booked for the venue, please visit:  August 2025 Underfloor heating was introduced to the Corn Exchange and lobby area and works started to build the new staircase to the first floor. Brick House Conservation carried out extensive restoration works to the Coat of Arms in the Town Hall. This was gifted to the Town Council in 1893 and is the first time such works have taken place. Photos can be viewed on the Town Council’s Instagram and Facebook pages, but here are a selection of photographs taken during conservation. The before and after images are below: Before Photo After photo July 2025 Decorations to the Town  Hall. Works carried out to the toilets and changing rooms. June 2025 The Town Clerk took members of the community on a tour of the site on 5th June 2025. The birdcage scaffolding has been removed and the paintwork is starting to take shape and is looking fresh and welcoming! May 2025 The coat of arms from the Town Hall is being removed by a conservator for full restoration during the Summer months. Members of the Blandford-Mortain Twinning Association arranged for visitors from Mortain – Mr and Mrs Van Saene and the Mayor of Mortain’s daughter, Kory Desserouer – to attend the Mayor Making Ceremony in the Blandford Forum Parish Church this morning. We were thrilled they could join us! This was followed by a site visit Corn Exchange, Blandford Forum led by contractor, Peter Hammond and the Town Clerk, Linda Scott-Giles and they explained details of the scheme. Cllr Alan Cross, who is the Town Council representative to the Twinning Association invited our French guests back when the works are complete. April 2025 Stage roof carpentry works are complete and the stage ceiling plaster boarded skim coat and decorated. Decoration to main rafters within Corn Exchange has been carried out with the acoustic panelling installed. The lighting gantry has been installed to stage ceiling. High level fans within Corn Exchange installed. Slates to East elevation near complete, working in conjunction with lead installation to gantry support legs. Electrical 1st fix alterations to Town Hall toilets, Shambles, and Town Hall. Mechanical 1st fix to Shambles, stage dressing room and dressing room toilet. March 2025 The Town Council has received the £1.3m loan from the Public Works Loan Board. Councillors have agreed to reduce the term of the loan from 50 years to 25 years, in order to substantially reduce the repayment amount over the whole of the term. Two students from The Blandford School joined the Town Clerk for work experience week and were given a tour of the building site. February 2025 A site meeting has taken place with a contractor to quote for new stage curtains. Councillors approved the quote and they have been ordered. Here are some more fascinating photos of the progress being made in the Corn Exchange. 🏗 Stage roof – the first photo shows the roof has been raised, and the structural steels are now in place. To the right of the photo is the rear of the council office and to the left is the far end of the Corn Exchange hall (the boarding is in place to protect the glazed window of the crest). Below that, you can just about see the proscenium arch above the stage has been reinstated. Stage Roof Steels 🪚 Stage – the steps that used to lead up to the Corn Exchange stage have been removed and the apron of the stage has been brought forward into the hall. The photo shows the timberworks of the extension. This will help with performances and acoustics. Stage Extension 🔌 Shambles – you can see the Shambles slabs have been lifted to allow for the incoming electrical cable. Shambles 📦 Mezzanine Store – the photos show the masonry alterations forming the mezzanine store entrance and steel jams that form the opening to the store. We were in desperate need of more storage space and are making use of the void that was above the lobby toilets. Mezzanine January 2025 The Town Council approved restoration works to the George II Royal Coat of Arms in the Town Hall. This will be carried out by a local conservator. Thanks to The Georgian Group for the grant of £1,000 towards this work and to the Blandford Town Museum for their guidance with suitable contacts to carry out the works. The Coat of Arms was gifted to the Town Council in 1893, is in desperate need of restoration work and as it needs to be protected during this stage in the Corn Exchange works, hence it is the perfect time both logistically and financially to carry out the works. The builders are making efforts to protect the coat of arms, but it would be best to remove it for repair to ensure it won’t be damaged during works to the Town Hall. We have the following extracts from council minutes referring to the Coat of Arms: Council Minutes 18/07/1893 The Mayor informed the Council that Mr Frank Blanchard had offered a painting of the Royal Arms to the Council. It was resolved that the same be accepted and the Town Clerk was directed to convey to Mr Blanchard the best thanks of the Council for his present. Council Minutes 02/12/1920 As to the cleaning etc of the Town hall and down the stair cases it was decided not to have the electric light installed but it was decided to wall paper varnish the Coat of Arms, the ceilings to be whitewashed and patched clean and brush down the walls and to tart up the green paint. The Coat of Arms is shown in situ pictured below: Coat of Arms in the Town Hall Smoking through the ages is shown in the photo below – all these items were found by the contractor under the Corn Exchange floor! Smoking paraphernalia found under the Corn Exchange floor December 2024 Lead testing on the paint has been carried out. Cornice repair works in the Corn Exchange have continued. Padstones, steels and spreader plate installed in preparation for new Mezzanine steel, along with the installation of new steel introduced to support external wall and Mezzanine Floor. This Mezzanine level will be for the sound booth in the Corn Exchange (above the kitchen). Protection to the Bullseye window with stained glass within the Corn Exchange, as commencement of structural steels and roof timbers start in the new year. Protection to the Coat of Arms within the Town Hall. New flat roof to either side of the Town Hall glazed link roof constructed and base layer of ply and membrane installed. It was great to welcome Simon Hoare MP to the Corn Exchange on Friday 6th December, where he had the opportunity to see the impressive progress on site with Sara Loch, Sarah Manley and Linda Scott-Giles, Town Clerk. We are very grateful for Simon’s continued support and invaluable guidance in securing crucial funding streams that are helping bring this project to life.  You can view the photos from the visit here: Visit from Simon Hoare MP November 2024 The rooflight (lantern ceiling) in the Corn Exchange was installed and completed and the roof reinstatement works were started. The asbestos within the stage roof was removed.  The Corn Exchange cornice repairs were started. The Mayor, Cllr Noc Lacey-Clarke held his civic day inviting Mayors from across the county to Blandford Forum, with tours of the Corn Exchange and Parish Church. You can view some photos of the visit here: Mayor’s Civic Day October 2024 Works to the flooring in the lobby area and toilets have progressed and can be seen in the photos at this link:(20+) 👷‍♂️ Works to the Corn Exchange are… – Blandford Forum Town Council | Facebook September 2024 Residents will now be familiar with the white hoarding installed to the front of the Town Hall/Corn Exchange in June. A timeline banner has been installed to brighten up the area and provide information about the project and Blandford Forum’s history. To view the artwork, please click on the link below… Hoarding Artwork – Corn Exchange Timeline Banner August 2024 The artwork for the hoarding is complete and is due to be printed this month. You can view the files here: Timeline Plans and Project Summary Councillors, staff and local organisations visited the Corn Exchange on 15th August 2024 to view progress made with the: proscenium infill removed above the stage plaster lath ceiling and suspended ceiling removed from the stage Shambles stores removed Town Hall stairs removed disabled toilet and caretaker’s office and store removed floors removed in the lobby area and toilets commencement of lobby area walls July 2024 We are working on artwork for the hoarding. Removal of lobby flooring and staircase: Lobby floor removed Lobby floor removed Toilet floor removed Toilet floor removed Flooring and stairs to Town Hall removed Flooring and stairs to Town Hall removed Demolition works are well underway with the removal of the toilets and caretaker’s office/store in the lobby area, the door to the staircase leading to the Town Hall. Demolition of the accessible toilet and wall, along with the caretaker's store and office. The staircase leads up to the Town Hall and is also being removed. Demolition of the accessible toilet and wall, along with the caretaker’s store and office. The staircase leads up to the Town Hall and is also being removed. Demolition of the toilets Demolition of the lobby toilets Looking out from the Corn Exchange entrance to the lobby Looking out from the Corn Exchange entrance to the lobby Shambles render and ceiling removed, store rooms removed Shambles render and ceiling removed, store rooms removed The birdcage scaffolding in the Corn Exchange is incredible! It’s in place to gain access to the glazed ceiling. You can see in the photos that the fireplaces have been revealed, the awful trunking around the mouldings has gone, and the steps to the stage have been removed, exposing the steps that lead underneath the stage. Birdcage scaffolding from the Corn Exchange stage Birdcage scaffolding from the Corn Exchange stage Birdcage scaffolding from the stage looking down the side to the tiles, moulding and fireplace Birdcage scaffolding from the stage looking down the side to the tiles, moulding and fireplace Birdcage scaffolding looking up to the mouldings where the trunking has been removed Birdcage scaffolding looking up to the mouldings where the trunking has been removed Photo showing the removal of the stage steps, exposing the steps leading underneath the stage Photo showing the removal of the stage steps, exposing the steps leading underneath the stage June 2024 Town Council staff continue to seek funding for various elements of the project. The hoardings went up around the bollarded area outside the Town Hall: Hoarding from the front Hoarding from the front Hoarding from the Spar side Hoarding from the Spar side May 2024 The contractor took over the site on Monday 20th May 2024. Staff have settled into the Legion building. April 2024 – Town Hall / Corn Exchange Decant and Office Relocation The Town Council closed the building to the public on 1 April 2024, with the contractors – Hammond Building Contractors – due to start in late April/early May, and staff have been decanting to various locations since. The offices are being relocated to the Royal British Legion building, also on Church Lane, for the duration of the works. The proximity to the existing office is ideal, it is an accessible building and it will provide two offices for staff to work from. We will continue to be open to the public Monday to Friday between 9:30am to 12:30pm. The Legion has also kindly agreed to accommodate the Citizens Advice service currently located in the Town Council office, which is fantastic news that we can continue to be located together and residents can continue to access this essential service. Town Council meetings have moved to Woodhouse Gardens Pavilion, instead of the Corn Exchange, on a Monday evening and the calendar of meetings can be found here. The Corn Exchange’s Indoor Market and popular Bethell Bridge café have relocated to Woodhouse Gardens on a Thursday. Please continue to support them and help us keep the Indoor Market going. March 2024 – Loan Application Outcome and Funding Secured Infogram for the project update On Monday 11th March 2024, the Town Council was informed that its application for a loan of £1.3 million to the Public Works Loan Board was approved by the Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities. This is the final piece in the jigsaw of providing funds for the renovation of the Town Hall and Corn Exchange. Other funding sources are summarised below: Income External Funding Charitable Trust	 £           400,000 Community Ownership Fund	 £           265,000 Dorset Council retained s.106	 £           264,810 Stubhampton Estate	 £           100,000 Dorset Council additional s.106	 £             40,320 Dorset Capital Leverage Fund	 £             25,000 Pilgrim Trust	 £             20,000 Other private donations	 £               2,000 Internal Funding BFTC Corn Exchange reserve	 £           861,500 BFTC Corn Exchange reserve 24/25	 £             30,000 BFTC additional reserves	 £           180,000 Sub-total income	 £        2,188,630 Costs Estimate of outstanding consultancy costs to project completion	 £           200,000 Fee contingency	 £             50,000 Preferred works contract price	 £        3,144,633 New electrical supply	 £             50,000 Insurance costs to cover the works	 £             20,382 Storage costs	 £               5,000 Office relocation costs	 £             15,000 Stage curtains	 £             15,000 Furniture for completed venue	 £             10,000 Hoarding artwork	 £               1,000 Sub-total costs	 £        3,511,016 Current Shortfall (difference between costs and income)	 £        1,322,386 Maximum loan amount approved by Public Works Loan Board	 £        1,300,000 Difference between costs and income	 £             22,386 Sub-total income including loan amount	 £        3,488,630 The original tender price was £3,303,973 and we have been working with the contractor and the quantity surveyor on a value engineering process. As funding streams are secured, we are able to bring as much as possible from the tender back into the project. The more match-funding we secure, the lower the loan amount we require, which will save a considerable amount.

D7 Omnibus Restoration   Midland Red D7 restoration appeal Don't Delay Donate Today! Donate Wave-Line.png d7.png Transport Museum Wythall has started a new challenge, the restoration of Midland Red D7 double decker number 4482, the only existing D7 with a viable restoration opportunity. The 350 D7s could be found throughout Midland Red's large territory and were very much a 'work horse' especially in the Black Country. Although on display in the museum and looking ready for the road, 4482 is hiding significant 'under the skin' structural issues, beyond our ability to fund from current museum resources, therefore preventing its return to the road. The plan is in place for a full restoration to running condition, offering public rides from our base at Wythall and joining our recently restored LD8 and other icons from the Midland Red fleet. This appeal for funding will speed up the restoration programme, enabling this interesting Birmingham-built vehicle to delight enthusiasts and families alike, out on the road. Contributions can be made by clicking the link below. All donations are welcome but the names of those offering over £100 will be carried on a commemorative plaque carried inside the vehicle. Those contributing £500 and above will be allocated a seat on a special 'first public appearance' run of the vehicle - we plan to invite a celebrity to join us on this first run. We estimate that c£75,000 will complete the restoration, most of which is attributed to the remanufacture of the body structure (which will require a complete body strip) and its refinishing. Fortunately the vehicle is in good mechanical condition and will require only a 'light' overhaul along with some retrimming. 4482 history Midland Red was unusual in designing and assembling its own buses and coaches, many important parts being manufactured for the company in Birmingham and the Black Country, such as chassis members by Rubery Owen. Indeed, the engines were unique in that they were designed and manufactured 'in house'. One of its major suppliers of bodywork at the time was Metropolitan-Cammell of Birmingham. Most of the opportunities for weight saving lay in the bus bodies, simpler construction methods being combined with plainer interiors. Metropolitan-Cammell was the clear leader in the introduction of lightweight bus bodies and launched its 'Orion' body at the 1952 Commercial Motor Show. The first production bodies were supplied to bus operators in the late summer of 1953. Among them were Midland Red D7 class buses with bodies built on 'Orion' principles but with Midland Red's own and unique styling ideas. Midland Red was arguably the most enthusiastic early supporter of lightweight bodies, immediately declaring an intention to construct 373 D7 buses (in practice only 350 were built). The D7s defied critics of lightweight bodies by lasting as long as their heavier predecessors, a life of 15 years being typical, although life extension beyond that was arguably optimistic. The final withdrawal of the type came in 1973 with our particular example which had operated from a range of Midland Red garages both 'city' and 'rural' from 1956 to 1971. After a variety of private owners, including Aston Martin, it arrived in a somewhat tired condition at the Transport Museum Wythall in 1979. There it was used lightly to attend rallies but was taken out of use in 1982 principally because of its poor body structure condition. It received a cosmetic restoration in 2004 to enable it to be statically displayed in the museum. ​Only a handful of D7's survived in to preservation, the greater majority have now fallen by the wayside and XHA482 is now the only one capable of economic restoration. If restoration is delayed much longer, many of the generation who grew up with them will not be around to enjoy riding on one in service again.

Knutsford Market Hall Funds awarded for Market Hall Renovation Knutsford Town Council > News > Town Council News > Funds awarded for Market Hall Renovation 26 Sep A CGI render of the revised frontage to the market hall Plans to renovate Knutsford Market Hall have taken a significant step forward with the announcement of major grant funding to help deliver the project. Knutsford Town Council has secured a grant of £560,000, 80% of the estimated £700,000 costs of undertaking a complete refurbishment of the market hall. The grant comes from the Community Ownership Fund, a £150m fund from the UK Government as part of its Levelling Up agenda. The fund aims to help communities save cherished local assets and the Market Hall is one of a number of projects awarded funding across the country. The project is at an early stage, with more detailed plans to be developed and announced in the coming weeks ahead of a planning application being submitted by the end of the year. Once a timeline for the works has been agreed, the council will be able to determine how to accommodate traders whilst works take place. The renovations will include a complete refurbishment of the interior of the market hall, a redesigned frontage to create a more open and welcoming space and replacement of the stall structures creating more storage for traders within each stall. The works will also deliver significant improvement to the energy efficiency of the building including installing insulation, closing doors and solar panels. Speaking of the award, Town Clerk Adam Keppel-Green said “It was incredible to receive the news of our success in securing this game-changing level of funding for the Market Hall. Thanks to the Community Ownership Fund, we will be able to deliver a significant improvement to the Market Hall in 2024, securing its future for decades to come”. Knutsford Market Hall was built in 1964, and with the exception of minor works in the 1980s, remains largely the same building as it was almost 60 years ago. Town Mayor, Cllr Peter Coan said “Knutsford Market Hall is a fantastic space for small businesses and a vital part of our high street, we are delighted to be able to celebrate its 60th Birthday in 2024 by giving it a new lease of life” The Community Ownership Fund provides grants of up to 80% of the project costs. The council is currently exploring how to provide the balance with options including use of its capital reserve and borrowing. The council is also seeking a further grant through the Shared Prosperity Fund. This round of the Community Ownership Fund was the first to be open to Town and Parish Councils, thanks to the lobbying work of the National Association of Local Councils (NALC) and Society of Local Council Clerks (SLCC), and Knutsford is one of nine local councils that has secured funding in this round. Plans for Knutsford's £700k market revamp scrapped 10th October 2024 Leisure Local government Planning and development Shopping Knutsford By Barbara Jordan Senior Reporter Share 1 Comment Plans to revamp Knutsford Market Hall have been scrapped (Image: Supplied) Plans to revamp Knutsford Market Hall have been scrapped (Image: Supplied) AMBITIOUS plans for a £700,000 refurbishment of Knutsford Market Hall have been scrapped after the scheme went over budget. Knutsford Town Council had hoped to give the Silk Street market a new lease of life to celebrate its 60th anniversary this year. However, rising costs meant the project could not be delivered either on time or on budget. READ > Knutsford pub with luxury bedrooms The Freemasons Inn on sale for £2m Artist impression of the proposed new look for Knutsford Market Hall Artist impression of the proposed new look for Knutsford Market Hall (Image: Supplied) Cardiologist: The Simple Trick to Shed Belly Fat After 50 (It’s Genius!) Health Watcher | Sponsored The renovation was relying on a government grant which has been withdrawn because the council was unable to meet certain conditions. The interior of the market was to be given a complete refurbishment and a modern glass frontage was set to create a brighter more welcoming image. Traders, preparing to move to temporary premises at 60 King Street during the makeover, had been looking forward to the improvements. Knutsford Market Hall celebrates its 60th anniversary this year Knutsford Market Hall celebrates its 60th anniversary this year (Image: Supplied) Town mayor Cllr Colin Banks said: “This is obviously extremely disappointing news for our market traders, the council and the market community - especially after the excitement of the prospect of a more significant investment. “We will now strive to deliver the best possible improvement to the Market Hall with available funds and aim to ensure our Market Hall can continue to provide a home to small businesses in our town centre.” The Town Council was awarded a £560,000 grant in the first round of the government’s Community Ownership Fund. A further grant of £160,000 came from the Shared Prosperity Fund, managed by Cheshire East Council. The funding application was based on initial costings developed by quantity surveyors to the outline design. Town Council clerk Adam Keppel-Green said: “During the last 12 months this project has been progressing through the detailed design and tender stage. “Tendering was concluded in September and the project came in significantly over budget and would have required around a further £200,000 to be found. “At the same time, there was a requirement to complete within a certain timeframe for both this funding and the £160,000 of funding provided through another fund which mean the planned project could not be delivered either in time or on budget. “Value engineering took place to develop a revised scheme that could be delivered within the funding envelope and time. “Unfortunately, the government did not approve the revised scheme or an extension to the grant period,and has withdrawn its funding. “ The Town Council will now be exploring what improvements to the Market Hall it can deliver with the £160,000 grant from the Shared Prosperity Fund. Town Mayor Cllr Colin Banks said: “This is obviously extremely disappointing news for our market traders, the council and the market community - especially after the excitement of the prospect of a more significant investment. “We will now strive to deliver the best possible improvement to the Market Hall with available funds and aim to ensure our Market Hall can continue to provide a home to small businesses in our town centre.” The proposed new layout would have featured a central hall to allow community use when the market is closed.

Project_name: Purchase of the freehold for The Centurion Pub
Text: Purchase of the freehold for The Centurion Pub   Share Offer Exciting News! The management committee are pleased to announce that at 4:30pm on Monday 21st October 2024, we completed the purchase of The Centurion Pub. After 8 years of being community run, the pub is now under full community ownership and this has helped to secure its future as a community asset for Vicars Cross. We are so pleased that we have achieved this, after a massive number of hours and work have been put into making this happen, as well as encountering various hurdles at different stages of the legal process. You may have been aware that when we were awarded the grant from the Community Ownership Fund in September 2023, we had 12 months to complete the purchase.  However, given delays we were faced with the legal tasks for the sale, COF did agree a short extension, given these were beyond our control. We want to thank every single person who has invested in this process and stuck with us along the way.  This includes people who bought shares, made donations, organised syndicates, donated via GoFundMe, delivered leaflets to raise awareness and shared information on social media As a community, you should all be very proud of what you have achieved. We will now be busy for the next couple of weeks preparing share certificates ready to be issued, and we will make further contact with everyone who has bought shares to advised of when the share certificates will be ready. We will also be making arrangements for a massive celebration event! The Management Committee

Haveringland Community Hub   Norfolk church to benefit from a share of more than £12m of government funding The money will go towards making the church more accessible St Peter's Church, Haveringland© Alamy St Peter's Church, Haveringland Author: Sian Roche Published 25th Sep 2023 Last updated 25th Sep 2023 A church in Norfolk's been declared a "cherished institution" and is receiving thousands of pounds of government funding. St. Peter's Church in Haveringland is one of forty-five treasured spaces in villages, towns and cities across the UK getting a share of more than £12.3m in funding, which the government says will enable them to be run by the community, for the community. £48,000 from the government's Community Ownership Fund is going towards St Peters, and will be used to install an accessible WC and kitchen, thus ensuring the future viability and sustainability of the site so it can continue to run its community hub. What other institutions are getting funding? Other notable institutions receiving funding including a historic Yorkshire railway bridge, which carries the railway line over Bridgehouse Beck between Keighley and Oxenhope, which will receive £1 million for an urgent repair. It will protect a tourist rail line which is used by more than 250,000 passengers a year, and improve transport infrastructure in the region so that the local economy can continue to thrive. The original bridge was built in 1867, and the railway line has featured in dozens of films and TV shows over the years including The Railway Children in 1970. An educational aerospace discovery centre in Kinross will be created with £300,000 from the fund, offering interactive learning opportunities and exhibitions which will inspire future generations to pursue STEM careers. The Vale of Aeron pub in Ystrad Aeron, Wales, which was known as a favourite haunt of the legendary poet Dylan Thomas, has been given £300,000 for renovations to keep it open and make it accessible to the whole community. "Keeping them going is vital for supporting communities" Jacob Young, Minister for Levelling Up, said: “Our priority is to support communities and deliver opportunities right across the country, which is why we’re investing £12.3m to secure the future of cherished community institutions. “These places – from pubs to historic railway lines – are the golden thread which run through our social fabric, and keeping them going is vital for supporting communities.”   Haveringland Community Hub Photo of church credit: Richard Flagg Haveringland Church – Community Hub – Heritage Centre Haveringland Together, which is the name of the “Friends of St. Peter’s Church”, arranges events which celebrate the church in its roles as a festival church and a community hub and also events which promote our local heritage. Funds raised at these events are used to maintain, repair and improve St. Peter’s Church.   Haveringland Chronicles Haveringland Chronicles Haveringland Chronicles Haveringland Hall Haveringland Village RAF Swannington St. Peter’s Church William of Norwich Repairing the past – building the future – darkness to light photo of church with field of poppies in front From a distance, St. Peter’s Church stands as proudly as it did following its rebuilding by Lord de Ramsey at the end of the 19th century. However, take a closer look and the need for a major repair project becomes evident. Outside, against the walls, roof tiles are lined up, awaiting reinstatement. Look closely and you can see grass growing in the walls where the gutters have failed. Inside walls are damp and cracked, even the wooden roof has been affected by water ingress. As you can see in the photos below, the weathering over the centuries as well as the feet of numerous worshippers have left their mark. St. Peter’s Church has been closed for nine months in 2024 whilst building works were undertaken to address these issues. Here’s a link to more details about the “Building the future – repairing the past” project as well as more photos of the continuing project: Haveringland Parish Church – Building the future – repairing the past Darkness to light – 16th December 2024 Photo: Surrounded by darkness an almost full moon shines above the floodlit tower of St. Peter’s Church On the 16th December 2024 a special service was held to celebrate the reopening of St. Peter’s Church. The Bishop of Lynn, the Right Reverend Dr Jane Steen, addressed full pews. During the service she rededicated the church following its nine month closure for extensive repairs, restoration and improvements. For the first time in many years the church is watertight. It is now wheelchair – accessible and has new facilities including heating, lighting, an accessible toilet, a small kitchen and a high-quality audio and video system. A new interactive “Heritage Hub” means visitors can discover more about the history of Haveringland and St. Peter’s Church. There is even a self-guided smartphone tour. From early 2025, once the improvement work is fully complete, the church will be open during daylight hours. Here’s a link to the report on the Diocese of Norwich website: Service heralds reopening of Haveringland Church – Diocese of Norwich The photos show the order of service, Bishop Jane and Reverend Andrew, a candlelit window and the path to the church. Photos: Lorna Wilson

Keep Overton Swimming   Over Wallop and Overton community facilities get significant funding 25th September 2023 National government Planning and development Overton By Toby Oliver Reporter @JournoToby Share 0 Comments 3 A SWIMMING pool and community pavilion have been given significant funding to improve their facilities. Over Wallop Parish Council was awarded £266,500 and Lordsfield Swimming Club, in Overton, was awarded £835,400 in the latest round of levelling up funding. Forty-five treasured spaces in villages, towns and cities will receive more than £12.3m in funding so they can be run by the community, for the community. Thanks to the latest funding from the government’s Community Ownership Fund, these institutions will be owned and run by hardworking community groups. READ MORE: Expensive Andover country home on sale for £1.5m on Zoopla In Over Wallop, the funding will be used to rebuild the dilapidated community pavilion and create a sustainable sports facility and community hub for residents of the village and surrounding rural area. Andover Advertiser: Lordsfield Swimming Club In Overton, it will be used for essential renovations that will create a long-term, sustainable future for the community swimming pool so the volunteer-run facility can continue to provide low-cost swimming to local schools and the wider community. Jacob Young, Minister for Levelling Up, said: “Our priority is to support communities and deliver opportunities right across the country, which is why we’re investing £12.3m to secure the future of cherished community institutions. “These places – from pubs to historic railway lines – are the golden thread which run through our social fabric, and keeping them going is vital for supporting communities.” The Community Ownership Fund has now given £49.3m for 195 projects across the UK. This includes almost £8m for 33 pubs so that local people can still go to their beloved local, and £14.6m for 66 community centres so they can continue to play an important role in people’s lives. Andover Advertiser: Over Wallop pavilion The latest round of allocations includes £770,057 for three projects in Wales, £992,825 for four projects in Scotland and more than £1m for four projects in Northern Ireland. Changes which came into effect for this round of bids also meant that all projects could bid for up to £1m in funding, not just sports clubs, and the amount organisations needed to match fund decreased to only 20 per cent, to allow more places to benefit. The Community Ownership Fund is currently open again for bids and will close on Wednesday, October 11, and groups are being urged to apply for up to £2m in funding for the very first time.   Overton Radio Saturday 24th May 2025 at 15:23 Janice Stott officially re-opens Overton's £1.2m Lordsfield Swimming Club. More information about the refurbishment project, it's amazing volunteers and the club's hopes for the future coming soon to Overton Radio. Congratulations everyone.

Purchase of Leybourne Woods   Leybourne Parish Council Secures £120,000 Community Ownership Fund Grant to Preserve Leybourne Woods for Generations to Come 26th September 2023 / Parish Clerk Leybourne Parish Council is thrilled to announce that it has successfully secured, in principle, a £120,000 grant from the Community Ownership Fund to purchase and preserve Leybourne Woods for the benefit of the local community, both current and future generations. This significant achievement marks a pivotal moment in the council’s commitment to enhancing the natural environment and ensuring that this precious green space remains accessible to all. The Department of Levelling Up Housing and Communities positive consideration and approval of our proposal has been instrumental in realising this vision. We extend our heartfelt gratitude to them for recognising the importance of preserving Leybourne Woods for the community’s wellbeing and enjoyment. Kevin Wagstaff, Chairman of Leybourne Parish Council said, “This remarkable accomplishment would not have been possible without the unwavering support of the residents of Leybourne. We want to express our sincere appreciation to all who voted for the current council during the election and those who passionately signed the petition, demonstrating the strength of our collective dedication to protecting our local environment. The successful bid and grant acquisition were the results of the tireless efforts of our dedicated staff and councillors who worked diligently to make a compelling case for the acquisition of Leybourne Woods. Their commitment to preserving this natural treasure for the community is truly commendable, and their hard work has paid off.” As we move forward, Leybourne Parish Council is committed to ensuring that Leybourne Woods continues to thrive as a place of natural beauty, recreation, and community connection. We will work closely with local stakeholders to develop a comprehensive plan for the sustainable management and enhancement of this invaluable asset. The acquisition of Leybourne Woods is a testament to what can be achieved when a community comes together with a shared vision. We are excited about the future possibilities and the positive impact this cherished space will have on our community for years to come.   Leybourne Woods secured by parish council after purchase using government grant Cara Simmonds csimmonds@thekmgroup.co.uk Published: 15:59, 26 September 2023 |  |  |  | Members of a parish council say they are thrilled they have saved an area of ancient woodland. Leybourne Woods will now be preserved for generations to come after it was bought using a government community grant. Leybourne Woods will be purchased by the parish council using a government grant. Picture: Rightmove Leybourne Woods will be purchased by the parish council using a government grant. Picture: Rightmove The 21 acres of woodland is between Birling Road and the A228 Leybourne and West Malling bypass. In April, campaigners launched a petition to save the ancient site after it was put up for sale for £150,000. Many called on the parish council to buy the land and guarantee its protection. It was confirmed in June this had happened but was subject to contract and confirmation of funds. The parish council has now been able to officially purchase the land, after securing £120,000 from the government’s Community Ownership Fund – issued by the Department of Levelling Up Housing and Communities. Campaigners launched a petition to save the ancient site Campaigners launched a petition to save the ancient site It was announced yesterday (September 25) that 45 spaces across the UK will receive more than £12.3m. The grants will help local projects so they can continue to play an important role in people’s lives. Among those was Dartford Gym and Youth Club, also known as Europa Gym, which received £244,920, as well as The Margate School, which was awarded £400,000. Leybourne Parish Council chairman Kevin Wagstaff said: “This remarkable accomplishment would not have been possible without the unwavering support of our residents. “We want to express our sincere appreciation to all who voted for the current council during the election and those who passionately signed the petition, demonstrating the strength of our collective dedication to protecting our local environment. The woods were put up for sale for £150,000 earlier this year The woods were put up for sale for £150,000 earlier this year “The successful bid and grant acquisition were the results of the tireless efforts of our dedicated staff and councillors who worked diligently to make a compelling case for the acquisition of Leybourne Woods. “Their commitment to preserving this natural treasure for the community is truly commendable, and their hard work has paid off.” The parish council says it is committed to ensuring that the woodland “continues to thrive as a place of natural beauty, recreation, and community connection”. Members plan to work closely with local stakeholders to develop a comprehensive plan for the sustainable management and enhancement of the area.

Holton Pits - Community Open Space   Our story: Holton Pits is a 20-acre open space in Suffolk, UK, which has a mosaic of areas – including a large fishing lake, open acid grassland, wooded paths and sandy cliffs. For decades these 20 acres of open space have been enjoyed and a home for many. It’s a lovely space that is used regularly – dogs and people love it for walks and it is a great area for kids to explore. A space that is essential to the local community and improves the well-being of locals and visitors alike. As a former quarry the site is known by all, locally as 'Holton Pits' – it was a surprise when it was suddenly put up for sale by its owner, CEMEX, in September 2022. In response, the Holton Pits Preservation Group was formed to try and prevent the site from being sold and developed in a manner that might threaten its fragile biodiversity and restrict access for the local community. The new group were quick to nominate the site as an Asset of Community Value, and this status was granted by East Suffolk Council in November 2022. The group now constituted more formally as the Holton Pits Community Interest Company. After lots of support and fundraising from the local community, everyone was very pleased that the bid put forward by Holton Pits CIC to buy the site was accepted in May 2023. In November 2023 the sale was completed. The funds came from generous donations, fundraising activities, and the government's Community Ownership Fund.

Project_name: The Oxted ‘Park Hub’ Enhancing Impact and Securing the Future of Master Park
Text: The Oxted ‘Park Hub’: Enhancing Impact and Securing the Future of Master Park   Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted Wednesday, 1 May, 2024 Local News Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted (1) Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted (2) Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted (3) Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted (1)Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted (2)Claire visits the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted (3) I am a self-confessed community centre superfan. Community spaces can do wonders for our sense of belonging and our wellbeing, and I was thrilled to visit the site of the long-awaited Master Park Pavilion in Oxted recently. Following a mammoth fundraising effort, including securing £1.86 million from Your Fund Surrey and £250,000 from the Government’s Community Ownership Fund, the new community pavilion is well on its way to completion, and it was fantastic to get a sense of how community groups and sports clubs will be able to use the space when it is finished. Pammy, Phil, Roger, and the rest of the Master Park Trustees have done such a brilliant job getting the project off the ground – and I look forward to heading back when the Pavilion opens to the community towards the end of the year.   Work will continue for the best part of a year and we will be open for Bookings February 2025 The Park remains open and football and cricket will continue. We hope to keep the level of disruption to a minimum but we ask for your patience during this period. Should you have any questions or concerns then please call 07908 014525

Moor Imagination Centre, Buckfastleigh   Moor Imagination Collective Monday 25th September 2023 at 15:13 ***EDIT: This post seems to be doing the rounds again but was actually celebrating our community ownership success back in September 2023! *** We are thrilled to be able to share with you that our most recent application to the Community Ownership Fund was successful, and we are now able to proceed with purchasing Buckfastleigh Methodist church to create a community hub for our town. This has been a massive piece of work, involving extensive community consultation and business planning, and we are extremely grateful for the support of the many people who have helped us get to this stage 🙌 (Link to the most recent iteration of our business plan in the comments if you would like to find out more...)   About MIC what we cannot imagine cannot come into being bell hooks all about love: new visions Moor Imagination Collective (MIC) is a community interest company established in March 2022. It was set up to secure the former Methodist church in Buckfastleigh for transformation into a community hub. So far so good. Imagination is a powerful force for change – it empowers and inspires local people of all ages to shape a better future. front entrance and door to the Moor Imagination Centre, Buckfastleigh, Devon Our mission To enable resilient and regenerative community in Buckfastleigh Our aims To co-create a supportive and inclusive space To cultivate connections among us To be champions for creativity and to inspire imagination To celebrate our diversity through education, arts and wellness activities Our people Co-founder Directors Sophie Lovett (changemaker, writer, educationalist) James Lucas (changemaker, co-founder Bristol Bike Project and Totnes Bike Hub) Directors Alexandra Brown (public arts, events, marketing) Carly Hobson (charity, volunteers) James Perrott (social enterprise, finance) Community projects Our commitment to community heritage projects for Buckfastleigh is a central aim of MIC, which brings us direct funding. In 2024 as our doors opened to the public, our heritage projects began. Members of the community co-designed and led workshops and open-access creative activities for community weaving acorss the town (Moor Threads). Our first oral storytelling sessions were also set up, with local people recording history and memories. Members of the community also began a conversation about what future we can imagine for the MIC centre. Community archive Our ambition is to be a creative hub that will inspire, preserve and celebrate Buckfastleigh and its rich heritage. This includes the building of a town archive – a deep record for how we live, what we imagine and how we create together. As we change and grow, we will add images, words, films, sound and other outputs to the archive. In our hallway, just inside the main doors, we have created a little library that anyone can visit and use while they are in the building. We also welcome suggestions for what belongs here as we build our community collection. Members, circles and more Members Our members share our day to day tasks and are mostly our volunteers and staff. They become members in order to be more involved in the running and the direction of Moor Imagination Collective (MIC). Anyone who is employed by MIC or who volunteers regularly (after first volunteering 10 times) can apply to become a member. Show more Circles Our way of working together is based on a system of circles, each with different roles and responsibilities. Within a circle, everyone can play an active part in our work. The Mission Circle and the Operational Circle are the teams with the widest responsibility. As we grow and continue to focus in new directions, we will create circles for different departments. Our approach to leadership is inspired by Sociocracy and Holacracy as well as Frederic Laloux’s concept of Teal organisations. Our goal is to live today the future we want for tomorrow. Our business practices reflect this. Show more Find out more about circles Our funders Teal-coloured logo stamp of the National Lottery Heritage Fund, the major funder to the Moor Imagination Collective CIC that purchased the old Methodist Church to renovate the building and open a community creative hub in Buckfastleigh, Devon The National Lottery Heritage Fund The National Lottery Heritage Fund is the largest funder for the UK’s heritage. Using money raised by National Lottery players we support projects that connect people and communities to heritage. Our vision is for heritage to be valued, cared for and sustained for everyone, now and in the future. From historic buildings, our industrial legacy and the natural environment, to collections, traditions, stories and more, heritage can be anything from the past that people value and want to pass on to future generations. We believe in the power of heritage to ignite the imagination, offer joy and inspiration, and to build pride in place and connection to the past.

Whetstone Community Hub   Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png A vision for an inclusive community  space and a hub of disability support  in the heart of  Blaby District. To mark our 125th year, we are excited to announce our vision to create an all-encompassing hub for the local disabled community in the heart of Blaby District in Whetstone, Leicestershire. Embarking on this project on the 125th anniversary of the charity aims to act as a catalyst to further our work in enhancing the lives of disabled people by creating spaces where disabled communities can thrive. ​ The three buildings on site promise to become an all-encompassing hub for the local disabled community in the heart of Blaby in Whetstone, Leicestershire. Here are just some of the ways the chapel, community buildings and the manse are set to enhance the lives of disabled people. Get Involved Donate Care. Support. Community. The Chapel A vibrant space for community, engagement, involvement, and support. We want to create a place where everyone is welcome, a place of community spirit, work, and volunteering opportunities, and above all else positive impact for all. ​ Some of the events and services to run from the chapel include: Exhibitions and Events Information Services Outreach Projects ​Support Groups ​ Vocational Training The location of the Whetstone Baptist Church is particularly significant, as it has been a staple in the community for many years. We're committed to preserving the historic nature of the building while modernising it to meet the needs of disabled people and the wider community. ​ Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png Together, we can do so much. The Community Buildings We'll be working tirelessly to transform the community buildings into truly accessible and equipped spaces, complete with sensory rooms and changing places, for the delivery of care and support services for disabled people. ​ The buildings will compliment our Head Office and become another home to Mosaic 1898's Adult Day Services and Children's groups. ​ The versatility of the space excitingly allows us to engage with the local community, schools, and businesses to do so much more - a hub of social good. ​ There's no place like home. The Manse An around the clock respite care facility providing sought after non-nursing short breaks for disabled people with complex needs. This purposeful provision will offer Leicestershire families and caregivers the support and means to take respite with peace of mind that their loved ones are well cared for. ​ We know first hand how much a place like this is needed in Leicestershire. A home away from home. ​ We're excited by the road ahead and we'd love to invite you to join us and be a part of the journey. Get in touch with us if you'd like to get involved in any way. Contact Us Whetstone Baptist Church - Front of Building in Mosaic 1898 Logo.png Thank you to our Funders. ​ We are incredibly grateful to our funders for supporting the Whetstone Hub Project at Mosaic 1898. Their generosity is more than financial, it’s a powerful investment in inclusion, community, and lasting change. Together, we're building a more accessible, connected, and compassionate world. Thank you for being part of this journey.   Whetstone Baptist Church Flooring Installation – Whetstone Baptist Church (New Build Project) We were proud to be involved in the new build of Whetstone Baptist Church, supplying and installing all floor coverings throughout the purpose-built facility. This exciting project gave us the opportunity to contribute to the creation of a welcoming, modern environment for worship, community events, and day-to-day church activities. From the early planning stages, we worked closely with the church and the main contractor to specify flooring solutions that would meet the demands of a busy, multi-use building. The brief required a combination of aesthetics, durability, and ease of maintenance — all while creating a warm and inviting atmosphere for visitors of all ages. We supplied and installed a range of high-performance products, including: Commercial-grade carpet tiles in the main worship space and meeting rooms, offering comfort underfoot and acoustic benefits. Safety vinyl flooring in circulation areas, entrances, and washrooms to ensure slip resistance and longevity. Durable vinyl wood-effect flooring in multifunctional spaces for a clean, modern finish. Our team carried out the full installation across the building, ensuring everything was delivered on time and to the highest standard in coordination with other trades on site. The result is a beautifully finished space, ready to serve the community for years to come. It was a pleasure to be part of a project that brings so much value to the local area, and we’re honoured to have contributed to such a meaningful new chapter for Whetstone Baptist Church.

Kimpton Pavilion Refurbishment Project Government awards £62k for Kimpton pavilion refurb 25th September 2023 National government Hitchin By Christopher Day Reporter ChrisDay96 Share 0 Comments Bim Afolami MP (right) with Cllr Ralph Muncer in Kimpton. (Image: Bim Afolami MP) Bim Afolami MP (right) with Cllr Ralph Muncer in Kimpton. (Image: Bim Afolami MP) The pavilion at Kimpton Recreation Ground is set for a makeover after the government awarded a £62,000 grant towards its redevelopment. Kimpton Parish Council had applied for the money from the government's Community Ownership Fund, as they seek to refurbish the building. The Fund is awarding £150 million over four years to community groups who can take ownership of local assets that are at risk of being lost. Kimpton Pavilion was originally constructed in 2006 as a football clubhouse, and the refurbishment will provide a fitness studio, changing room, toilets with disabled accessibility, and an opportunity to set up a café. If you need to kill time on your computer, this game is a must in 2025! Top Strategy Game 2025 | Sponsored The government grant follows North Herts Council's decision earlier this year to award £1,000 to Kimpton Parish Council for the refurbishment. Bim Afolami, the MP for Hitchin and Harpenden, had supported the bid for government funding by writing to Michael Gove, the minister for levelling up, housing and communities. Mr Afolami said: “It is absolutely brilliant news for everyone in Kimpton that we’ve been able to secure this funding to renovate the pavilion on the Recreation Ground. "This is levelling up in action – and it is fantastic that the government have seen the potential that this project can have to really deliver for people in Kimpton. Kimpton Parish Council can be very proud of the hard work that they have put in to produce a really strong bid for funding. “I look forward to seeing the re-developed pavilion once it is delivered!” Cllr Ralph Muncer, who represents Kimpton ward on North Herts Council, said: “I’m delighted the government has awarded Kimpton Parish Council a sizeable grant towards the costs of refurbishing the pavilion, which has been in a state of disrepair for a number of years. "The government’s Levelling Up policy is all about supporting our communities, and I know this funding will be key to ensuring the pavilion can once again be used as a hub for our vibrant community in Kimpton.” Cllr Alistair Willoughby, executive member for communities and partnerships at North Herts Council, said: “It is good to see central government helping us invest in our communities. "Back in the summer North Herts Council awarded a grant of £1,000 to Kimpton Parish Council to help improve the facilities at the Rec, which is extremely valued by the local community. "We’re excited to see the completed project bring a great community benefit to residents for many years to come and hope that other areas in North Herts will benefit from central government investment in the future.”

Over Wallop Community Pavilion Over Wallop celebrates funding to rebuild pavilion 10th October 2023 Planning and development Hampshire Romsey By Toby Oliver Reporter @JournoToby Share 0 Comments From left: Cllr David Drew, Caroline Nokes, Kate Dixon and Cllr Stewart MacDonald (Image: Caroline Nokes / Facebook) From left: Cllr David Drew, Caroline Nokes, Kate Dixon and Cllr Stewart MacDonald (Image: Caroline Nokes / Facebook) VILLAGERS have welcomed news of funding of more than £250,000 to rebuild a cricket pavilion.
